skill.name: azure-messaging-webpubsubservice-py
skill.description: Guidance for using the Azure Web PubSub Service SDK for Python to authenticate clients, issue access tokens, send real-time messages, and manage groups and connections.
skill.summary: Use Azure Web PubSub Service from Python for real-time messaging, client tokens, groups, permissions, and async service calls.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, environment access, weak cryptography, and a critical heuristic combination. Manual review found these are Markdown installation commands, SDK examples, placeholder Azure endpoints, and environment-variable credential examples, not hidden executable skill logic. No prompt injection, malicious network exfiltration, or confirmed weak cryptographic implementation was found.

low_findings.title: Fenced Installation Commands Flagged as Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections occur inside Markdown code blocks showing pip install commands and Python SDK snippets. They are documentation examples, not code executed by the skill, so the command execution finding is a false positive. Users should still review package installation in their own environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious lines are fenced Markdown examples and API operation names. No standalone script, hook, or instruction causes automatic shell execution.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Azure Endpoints and Environment Variables
low_findings.description: The network and environment-access findings come from Azure endpoint placeholders and use of an environment variable for the Web PubSub connection string. This is a normal SDK configuration pattern and does not send secrets to an unexpected third-party endpoint. The examples do print a generated client URL, so users should avoid exposing real tokens in logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoints use an Azure placeholder hostname and the environment variable is read for authentication. No evidence shows credential collection or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detector Has No Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography alerts point to the YAML description and a Markdown table header. No hash algorithm, cipher, custom token signing, or cryptographic implementation is present at those lines. This finding is dismissed as scanner noise.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive Markdown text, not cryptographic code. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithms.

low_findings.title: Critical Heuristic Combination Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The analyzer combined code-block commands, Azure URLs, and environment-variable examples into a critical heuristic alert. Manual review found no obfuscation, no prompt injection attempt, and no unauthorized data transfer behavior. The skill is a documentation reference for a network SDK, so the combined signal is not confirmed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains visible instructional examples only. Confidence is high, but the community source and credential-handling topic justify keeping a low residual risk rating.

content.user_title: Build Azure Web PubSub Python Messaging
content.value_statement: Python teams need clear patterns for real-time messaging with Azure Web PubSub. This skill provides focused examples for authentication, tokens, broadcasts, groups, permissions, and async calls.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install Azure Web PubSub service and client SDK packages.
Demonstrates service authentication with connection strings and DefaultAzureCredential.
Explains how to generate client access tokens with users, roles, and groups.
Provides examples for sending messages to all clients, users, groups, and connections.
Covers group membership, connection checks, disconnect operations, and permission grants.
Includes Python WebSocket client and async service client usage patterns.
limitations: Does not provision Azure Web PubSub resources or configure Azure permissions.
Does not validate production security settings, token lifetimes, or network policies.
Uses placeholder hub names, users, groups, and endpoints that must be replaced.
Does not include a runnable sample application or automated tests.

use_cases.title: Add Server Broadcasts
use_cases.description: Use Python service SDK examples to broadcast notifications or send targeted messages from an application server.

use_cases.title: Design Client Token Flow
use_cases.description: Map user IDs, roles, and groups before issuing client access tokens for WebSocket connections.

use_cases.title: Manage Live Connections
use_cases.description: Reference group, permission, and disconnect operations when building support or moderation workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Web PubSub Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the basic Python steps to create an Azure Web PubSub service client and send one message to all connected clients.

prompt_templates.title: Create Scoped Client Tokens
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a Web PubSub client token flow with user IDs, group access, and limited roles for a Python backend.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Group Messaging
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline Python service operations for adding users to groups and sending targeted group messages.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure Web PubSub Python design for token exposure, group permissions, reconnection handling, and safe logging practices.

output_examples.input: I need to broadcast a service alert from Python.
output: Create a WebPubSubServiceClient for your hub.
Authenticate with a connection string or managed identity.
Send the alert to all clients with a text or structured content type.
Avoid logging generated client access URLs in production.

output_examples.input: I need users to join only approved chat rooms.
output: Generate client tokens with a stable user ID.
Attach only the roles needed for approved groups.
Use group add and remove operations from trusted server code.
Revoke permissions when a user leaves or loses access.

output_examples.input: I want an async Python service integration.
output: Use the async WebPubSubServiceClient and async Azure identity credential.
Close both the service client and credential after operations finish.
Keep message formatting and error handling consistent with the synchronous path.

best_practices: Use managed identity where possible, and keep connection strings out of source control.
Grant narrow client roles and generate short-lived access tokens.
Use groups for targeted delivery and keep connection cleanup explicit.
anti_patterns: Do not print real client access URLs or connection strings in shared logs.
Do not issue broad send or join permissions to anonymous clients by default.
Do not rely on placeholder endpoints, hub names, or user IDs in production.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guide with Python and shell examples for developers to adapt manually.

faq.question: Which SDK does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers azure-messaging-webpubsubservice for server operations and azure-messaging-webpubsubclient for Python clients.

faq.question: Can it create Azure Web PubSub resources?
faq.answer: No. It does not include Azure CLI, ARM, Bicep, Terraform, or portal provisioning instructions.

faq.question: Does it support Microsoft Entra authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples include DefaultAzureCredential for service client authentication.

faq.question: Is it safe to log generated token URLs?
faq.answer: No. Generated client access URLs can contain sensitive tokens and should not be exposed in production logs.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
